entry.title: Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
entry.content:
Custom code migration during system conversion to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is usually complex and time-consuming process. The whole custom code developed in the SAP ECC systems since years must be assessed and analysed for SAP S/4HANA and finally adapted after system conversion.

Customers often face challenges to understand S/4HANA simplifications and their relation to the custom code due to scattered documentation across various channels like SAP simplification notes, SAP Help, blog posts. To find an answer to the specific SAP S/4HANA custom code migration question, one needs to go through multiple sources of information.

The IT project managers have a tough time to carry out the custom code migration projects on time and within budget because they experience difficulties during execution of their tasks like defining custom code migration scope, getting high-level understanding of custom code issues, creating effort estimations and project plans and finding the right development experts for adaptation. Ability to meet a decision whether to migrate custom code to S/4HANA or not assumes understanding of its business purpose which is quite challenging primarily due to the absence of documentation for legacy code. Custom Code Migration app on SAP BTP can execute custom code analysis for S/4HANA readiness, but S/4HANA comes along with more than 430 simplification items which affect custom code. Thus, to get at least a high-level understanding of issues reported by the Custom Code Migration app to assign appropriate work packages to custom code migration developers a project manager needs to deep dive into these lengthy simplification items and provided cookbooks, which is time- and effort-consuming activity.

Along with this custom code migration developers, who finally must adapt the code for S/4HANA need to understand the old legacy code in all details: not only the business logic, but also the technical processing flow which is quite tedious task due to missing documentation. ABAP test cockpit (ATC) in ABAP development tools for Eclipse can check custom code for S/4HANA readiness, but developers still invest high effort into studying related simplification items and cookbooks to understand the issues in detail. The ATC issues in custom code should be fixed ideally with a high automation rate, but the quick fixes provided for S/4HANA ATC issues in ABAP development tools for Eclipse can support about 40%-60% of the typical S/4HANA simplification use cases like conflicts with MATNR field extension or missing ORDER BY in SELECTs, the remaining ATC issues must be fixed manually by developers.

To support IT project managers and developers during custom code migration projects to speed up the analysis and adaptation tasks and enhance productivity the AI-based skills were delivered for custom code management tools within SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities. This will shorten the custom code migration projects timelines, reduce project risks and enhance capacity to meet deadlines and budget constraints.

Let’s look at them in detail.

AI-based custom code migration support for IT project managers

Starting with SAP BTP ABAP environment 2602 AI-based custom code analysis support for IT project managers is integrated in the Custom Code Migration app. To use it the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities must be activated in your SAP BTP ABAP environment system. See the SAP note 3571857.

Understand legacy code in the AI-based Custom Code Migration app

After execution of remote SAP S/4HANA readiness ATC check run over the custom code in the ECC system with the Custom Code Migration app in SAP BTP ABAP environment it is now possible under the Analyze Findings view to select ABAP program or ABAP class in the Findings table and use Code Explain capability to get explanation of its business purpose and processing logic.

You will get short and clear AI-based explanation of the code in the pop-up window, which will support your migration decision. Subsequently you can use Explain business purpose in detail and Explain processing logic in detail options to get more details to deepen the clarification.

Finally, you can use Scope part of the Custom Code Migration app to add or remove the object from scope based on your decision.

Understand S/4HANA ATC issues in the AI-based Custom Code Migration app

After execution of remote SAP S/4HANA readiness ATC check run over the custom code in the ECC system with the Custom Code Migration app in SAP BTP ABAP environment it is now possible under the Analyze Findings view to select an ATC issue in the Findings table and use ATC Explain capability to get an explanation of an S/4HANA related ATC finding. The explanation is provided in the pop-up window along with the proposed step-by-step resolution and contains also referenced S/4HANA simplification materials like simplification notes or cookbooks. Referenced documents are offered as clickable links within the AI-based response for navigation to further details.

This will enable you to grasp the root cause quickly without having to read through numerous SAP S/4HANA simplification information materials.

AI-based support for custom code migration developer in ABAP development tools for Eclipse

Starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 AI-based custom code analysis and adaptation support for developers is provided in ABAP development tools for Eclipse within SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities. To find out how to activate it see the blog post SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025.

NOTE: Code Explain for module pools and ABAP classes along with the deep dive explain (code in INCLUDEs and called methods outside of the source code object) will be delivered with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 FPS01.

Using Docs Chat for questions around custom code and S/4HANA simplifications

In most cases you as a custom code migration developer before starting with your adaptation activities first need to gain general understanding of S/4HANA simplifications related to your functional area of expertise to get an overview as well as deep dive into SAP standard changes relevant to your custom code.

Docs Chat capability utilizes SAP simplification notes, related technical information and SAP Help documentation to deliver based on this extensive input quick and accurate answers.

Select /docs topic in the Joule chat window and ask your question, for example “What are the data model changes in Sales and Distribution?”. Joule will list all technical changes along with affected objects including also reference links for further information. You can also ask follow- up questions for your query.

Now for example after finding out that VBUK and VBUP tables were eliminated in S/4HANA and knowing that this has direct impact on your custom code, you can ask Joule ”How can references to VBUK and VBUP be eliminated in my code?”. Joule will deliver short and concrete answer providing code proposals considering different use cases based on the information provided in the extensive cookbook about the VBUK and VBUP adaption.

Understanding of old legacy code

Generally, you as a custom code migration developer starting with your adaptation activities need to understand the old legacy code, which must be analysed and adapted for S/4HANA. Thereby you are confronted with the following problems: the code has been implemented many years ago, often expands over multiple pages, has very little or no documentation, the author of the code is not available any more for questions and so on. Thus you have a tedious and long-time task to digest this code to verify if it is still needed from the business perspective and understand it’s implementation logic for the adaptation.

The Explain for ABAP report and Explain for module pools as interactive Dynpro-based applications capabilities significantly speed up and make more accurate the task of understanding of old legacy code.

Just select an old ABAP report in the Project Explorer and use context menu Joule ->Explain. Joule will explain its business purpose and processing logic and will list all called objects. You can further deep dive into details using Explain business purpose in detail or/and Explain processing logic in detail features in the Joule chat window or ask Joule to explain called objects.

In the example below the business purpose of this custom ABAP program is to upload customer email addresses, which length exceeds 50 characters since the standard ABAP program cannot handle it, and this custom functionality will be still required in the S/4HANA system.

Therefore, you as developer can for example follow up on the details of processing logic to understand the program better for the upcoming adaptation.

You can also select the source code snippet in the editor and ask Joule to explain it:

Here is one example of the module pool explanation by Joule. In addition to the general overview and business purpose Joule describes user flow, which is very important for screen-based Dynpro applications to follow on the screen logic along with technical details for the processing logic of the screens (PBO/PAI) comprising deep-dive into INCLUDEs and Dynpro logic:

Understanding of S/4HANA related ATC findings

The basis for all custom code adaptation activities is the execution of S/4HANA readiness checks with ATC over custom code in your S/4HANA system in ABAP development tools for Eclipse. The ATC check result contains the list of S/4HANA incompatibility issues in custom code, which must be adapted, explaining the error and providing as a reference the link to the corresponding simplification information. The custom code migration developer needs to read through it to find out how to apply it to solve the error in the particular custom code object, which is time-consuming task.

The Explain ATC finding capability offers clear and short explanation of the S/4HANA related issue in custom code based on information provided in S/4HANA simplification items and cookbooks along with step-by-step recommendation on how to solve the issue.

After execution of the ATC check run with S/4HANA readiness check variant S4HANA_READINESS_2025 over your custom code the ATC findings are displayed in the ATC Problems View. In the Details of an ATC finding you see the description of the error, in this example “DB Operation found (KONV, see Note(s) 0002220005)” and the link to the related SAP simplification note 2220005, which must be examined to understand how to resolve this issue. Instead of doing this just select the ATC finding and use the context menu Joule->Explain.

Joule will deliver a crisp explanation telling you that the table KONV was replaced in S/4HANA with the table PRCD_ELEMENTS, which must be accessed using the new pricing result API. This explanation is based on the information provided within the cookbook about pricing conditions techniques attached to the related SAP simplification note 2220005. Joule can also provide step-by-step recommendations on how to fix the issue and can answer follow-up questions for further clarification. The referenced documents (related SAP simplification note in this example) are offered as clickable links within the Joule response for navigation to further details.

This will allow you to quickly grasp the root cause without having to read through numerous SAP S/4HANA simplification information materials.

Getting code proposal for adaptation of S/4HANA related issues

The final step for a custom code migration developer is to fix S/4HANA related ATC findings to adapt the code to S/4HANA.

The ATC findings which can be resolved using deterministic quick fixes are marked with the yellow light bulb in the ATC findings list. The resolution of the remaining findings must be done manually based on simplification information, which is quite laborious and time-demanding task.

The Code proposal capability allows you to fix the issue in custom code based on AI-based proposal emulating the same process as deterministic quick fixes and can be utilized for S/4HANA issues where deterministic quick fixes are not available.

After AI-based explanation of an ATC finding just click Generate Code Proposal in the Joule window

The AI-based code proposal is UI friendly: it can be generated either after explanation of the ATC finding (as suggested above) or from a source code line in the editor.

For generating code proposal from source editor just click on the ATC finding marker like this:

After Joule proposed the code change, you have different options. You can Copy the code proposal for pasting it into the editor later or Insert the code at the cursor position in the editor, or use Apply (recommended).

If selecting Apply, then in the same way as for deterministic quick fixes the Code Review Dialog will be shown displaying the original source code and the source code containing code proposal.

You as developer can review the code proposal and apply as needed. AI-based Code Proposal allows you to save the manual efforts and benefit from the semi-automated code adaptation as you already know it from the deterministic quick fixes.

Further information

Discover generative AI capabilities for ABAP custom code migration by watching the video: Unleash the power of GenAI for ABAP Custom Code Migration.

For more details about ABAP AI capabilities for custom code migration refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation Using Custom Code Migration AI assistant including also video tutorials.

entry.title: Calculating IBP Technical Week “Key Figure Date” in S/4HANA Using CDS View
entry.content:
When extracting data from S/4HANA into SAP IBP, a standard calendar week may span across two months.
IBP splits such weeks into “technical weeks”, and many key figures require the first date of the technical week.
while working on custom IBP integration for transactional data (key fugures), I discovered that S/4HANA does not provide this date natively.
So I created a very simple CDS view to derive it using the I_CalendarDate standard view.
 
Example week:
  • 29-Apr-2024 → 03-May-2024
  • Crosses April → May
  • IBP interprets this as:
    • Technical Week A: 29–30 April → Key Figure Date: 29-Apr
    • Technical Week B: 01–03 May → Key Figure Date: 01-May

The CDS view below calculates this value automatically for any date.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'IBP : Get Key Figure Date for any Date'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_IBP_Key_Figure_Date
  as select from I_CalendarDate
{
  key CalendarDate,

  @EndUserText.label:     'Key figure Date'
  @EndUserText.quickInfo: 'First day of the IBP Technical Week'
  case
    when cast( WeekDay as abap.int4 ) < cast( CalendarDay as abap.int4 )
    then dats_add_days( CalendarDate, -cast( WeekDay as abap.int4 ) + 1, 'INITIAL')
    else dats_add_days( CalendarDate, -cast( CalendarDay as abap.int4 ) + 1, 'INITIAL')
  end as KeyFigureDate
}

Result :

 

This CDS view can be used for :

  • Custom IBP extractors (CPI‑DS or RFC-based)
  • Time-based aggregation logic in Custom CDS views for IBP

Hope this will be helpful.

Regards,

Bharat Bajaj

entry.title: All that you love to know about SRMPROTOCOL
entry.content:
Please note that the term SRM used here stands for SAP Records Management.

Managing SAP database growth is a constant balancing act between system performance and compliance. If you’ve noticed the SRMPROTOCOL table climbing to the top of your "Top Growth" charts, you aren't alone. With some systems housing billions of records, understanding and managing this table is critical for a healthy SAP environment.

Here is a guide on what SRMPROTOCOL does, how to control it, and how to reclaim your space.

1. Understanding SRMPROTOCOL: The "Who, When, and What"

At its core, the SRMPROTOCOL table functions as the audit trail for SAP Records and Case Management. Whenever a case or record or document in SRM is created, modified, or deleted, the system generates an entry here to maintain a history of activity.

  • Audit Importance: This table is vital for legal and regulatory requirements. It ensures that businesses can track the lifecycle of a document or case for accountability.

  • Data Content: It stores the "SPS ID" (Service Provider Selection ID) and timestamps that link back to specific business objects.

Because these logs track every granular change, the table can grow exponentially in high-volume environments, eventually impacting database performance and backup times.

2. How to Control Log Growth

You don't have to let the table grow indefinitely. SAP provides built-in mechanisms to define how long these logs should live before they are eligible for removal.

Check Your Current Configuration

To see how your system is currently handled, follow these steps:

  1. Execute Transaction SRMREGEDIT.

  2. Locate the SPS ID relevant to your business (you can find these IDs within the SRMPROTOCOL table itself).

  3. Double-click the SPS Element ID.

  4. Navigate to the Classification tab to view the specific logging parameters.

Define "Log Keep Days"

Every organization has different audit requirements. SAP allows you to customize the expiry time for these logs. By setting a specific "retention period," you ensure that the system only flags entries for deletion once they are no longer legally or operationally required.

More details available in the Records Management Logging product documentation page.

3. Cleaning Up and Reducing Space

Once you have defined your retention policies, there are two primary methods to reduce the size of the SRMPROTOCOL table: Deletion or Archiving.

 

FeatureOption A: DeletionOption B: Archiving (Recommended for Compliance)
Primary ToolFM - SRM_PROTOCOL_DELETE_EXPIRY
SAP Note 795613

 If you are using Records Management as a standalone product, t-code ORGANIZER, consider referring to Archiving in Records Management to handle logs related to documents and records. Case Management: If you are using the framework of Case Management, t-code SCASE, consider referring to Archiving in Case Management for logs specifically tied to cases.

Data RetentionData is permanently removed from the system.

Data can be moved to long-term storage (external files).

Best Use CaseWhen logs are past their legal "Keep Days" and no longer needed.When logs must be kept for 5–10 years for audit/legal compliance.
System ImpactImmediate reduction in table row count; requires a "Reorg" to shrink disk files.High initial effort to set up, but provides a sustainable long-term solution.
Accessibility

Logs are deleted forever once the function module is processed.

Logs remain accessible via archive search tools if needed.

Handling Large Volumes

If you have a massive table, do not run the FM online. Instead, wrap the Function Module in a custom report and execute it as a background job to avoid timeouts and system strain.

Archiving is handled within the archiving framework. Each step is ideally scheduled as background job

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602

 
On February 14, the new 2602 release was delivered as the successor to the 2511 release. In this blog post, we provide a detailed overview of the new features and enhancements included in this release, highlighting the key improvements and innovations introduced with 2602. Some highlights in the new release include the My Home as your new personalized entry page in SAP Fiori Launchpad, the CDS Embedded Analytics Star Schema Generator and the RAP recommendations for input assistance based on the user input and interaction history. 

The list of all new features including more information can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. The new AI-related features are listed separately on the What's New for ABAP Generative AI in ABAP Cloud.

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • New Grouping Option Effect on transports in ATC Problems View
  • ABAP Testability: Enhancements to Test Double Frameworks (details)
  • Global User ID for ATC Exemptions
  • Displaying the Used Objects of CDS Service Definitions in the Relation Explorer

ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)

  • Collaborative Draft,  now also works in cross-business object scenarios if both business objects are collaborative draft-enabled, and if the cross-business object scenario is fully draft scope-enabled (details)
  • Recommendations, previously, business users had to fill out input fields manually and choose the right values. The new RAP recommendations standardize and ease this process: In RAP applications on an SAP Fiori user interface, RAP recommendations provide input assistance by proposing field values for input fields based on the user input and interaction history. RAP recommendations can be implemented using deterministic rules or using generative AI, which predicts the most likely values for each input field based on the context of the data. By integrating the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management, you can embed AI capabilities into the application, enriching traditional value help with context-aware suggestions (details)
  • Embedded Analytics Star Schema Generator, the Star Schema Generator Wizard streamlines the creation of star schema models using ABAP Core Data Services. It empowers you to build comprehensive analytical models with ease. This tool guides you through the entire generation process, ensuring a consistent and efficient workflow. The wizard supports the following key components: Transactional Scenario, Analytical Cube and Dimension Views (details)

Identity and Access Management

  • IAM App Assignment: So far, it was only possible to assign access to Fiori apps to business users indirectly via business catalogs. Now, it's also possible to directly create business roles based on IAM apps for SAP-delivered business catalogs
  • Maintain Business Roles, you can now directly assign IAM apps in the Maintain Business Roles app. This supports you in handling authorizations more flexibly. You can also use the Mass Change Wizard if you need to add or remove multiple IAM apps at once (details)
  • App References, the following apps now contain references to IAM apps: Maintain Business Roles, Manage Business Role, Changes After Upgrade, Display Restriction Types, Business Catalogs, Business Role Templates, IAM Information System (details)
  • Information System, the main entity Application is now deprecated. The successor will be the IAM app, which is now contained in the Main Entity dropdown list (details)
  • App Authorization Variant, you can now use the IAM app of the App Authorization Variant type. This gives you more flexibility in managing restrictions because it allows you to activate or deactivate app authorization variants to define different access rights per business role (details)

Manage API Snapshots

  • As a provider for released APIs, you can now download and upload API snapshots using the Manage API Snapshots app. This way, you can download API snapshots from the test system and upload them in the development system, thus preventing incompatible changes to your released objects early in the development process (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • My Home in SAP BTP ABAP environment (details)
  • Launching an App with a Transaction Code (details)
  • Download and Upload of Spaces (details)
  • Removal of Business Groups (details)

Landscape Portal

  • Publish Product: Publish up to Two Product Versions (details)
  • Deploy Product: System multi-select supported and performance improved (details)

Infrastructure

  • Optional Integration with SAP Key Management Service, SAP BTP ABAP environment provides an optional integration with the SAP Key Management Service (KMS). With this integration, it's possible to manage the SAP HANA Cloud database of an ABAP environment instance via SAP KMS (details)

entry.title: From Business Objects to Business Flows: The Missing Layer of RAP Documentation
entry.content:
Part 2 in a series on modern SAP documentation.

Part 1 covered Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices. This post tackles what happens when you need to understand how it all fits together.

In my last post, I argued that RICEFW documentation doesn't fit how we build with RAP. Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices give us a better way to document individual capabilities and the decisions behind them.

But I ended with an open question: what happens when someone asks “Walk me through what happens when a customer creates an order”? You point them to four BO Canvases. They read all four. They still don't understand the sequence — what triggers what, in which order, and what happens when something fails.

Functional Specs Describe Pieces, Not Flows

Our team documents in Confluence today. Functional specifications per RICEFW object — one for the report, another for the interface, another for the enhancement, another for the form. They're organized around technical artifacts, not business capabilities.

So if you want to understand the Sales Order, you're reading across half a dozen specs. The enhancement spec for validation, the interface spec for pricing, the form spec for order confirmation. Each makes sense on its own. But piecing together what actually happens when a customer creates an order means jumping between documents that were never designed to tell that story.

Questions like these are hard to answer:

What happens after the sales order is saved? What events fire, and in what order? If credit check fails, does the order stay blocked, or does it roll back? Which steps are synchronous and which are async? Where does pricing fit — before, during, or after save?

The answers live in people's heads, scattered across specs, or buried in code.

Why RAP Makes This Worse — and Better

In classic ABAP, process orchestration was often implicit. User exits, BAdIs, and function modules wired through transaction codes. You could trace the flow by reading the code.

RAP changes this. Business objects are decoupled. They communicate through events, EML calls, and service projections. Good architecture — but the process flow is no longer visible in any single place. The good news: because RAP forces you to think in events and clear BO boundaries, the sequence can be documented cleanly. You just need to actually do it.

An Idea: Sequence Maps

What I think we need is something simple — a visual flow that shows the chain of BOs in a business process, the trigger between each step, and whether it's synchronous or asynchronous. Nothing fancy. A diagram in Confluence: Customer creates order → Sales Order BO (SAVE) → [sync] Pricing determination → [sync] Credit check → [event] OrderCreated → [async] Inventory reservation → [async] Shipping notification

Even something this basic should be valuable. New developers could see the flow in thirty seconds. Architects could spot the sync/async boundaries. Application experts could validate whether the sequence matches what they expect.

The Full Picture

Each documentation layer answers a different question:

  • BO Canvas: What does this business object do?
  • ADR: Why did we build it this way?
  • Dependency matrix: What's connected to what?
  • Sequence map: What happens when a business process runs?

The sequence mapping is the part I haven't started yet — but it's the gap I feel most in daily work. Too many “go ask Henrik, he knows” conversations. If you're on a similar journey — moving from RICEFW specs to something that fits RAP — I'd like to hear what you've tried.

entry.title: Recurring Entry Approval Workflow in SAP S/4HANA - FBD1/FBD2/FBD9 Transactions
entry.content:
Introduction:

In many organizations, finance teams handle repetitive postings such as rent, subscriptions, insurance, lease payments, and service charges. Manually posting these entries every period increases operational effort and the risk of errors.

SAP provides the Recurring Entry Program (Transaction FBD1) to automate such postings. However, in controlled environments, organizations often require approval workflows before recurring documents are posted.

This blog explains how to implement and control a Recurring Entry Workflow using FBD1, ensuring automation with proper financial governance.

Requirement:

In SAP Standard, through FBD1 users can create recurring document templates. But these templates cannot be posted automatically. The created recurring document templates must be posted via F.14 transaction. It happens periodically through Job Scheduling.

FBD1 does not include any approval mechanism by standard. This leads to

  • Unauthorized financial commitments.
  • Incorrect Recurring Postings.
  • Audit, Compliance & Seggregation of Duties (SoD) violations.

To mitigate these risks, business requires an approval mechanism, so that

  • Authorized user only creates Recurring Document Templates (FBD1).
  • Delegation of Authority (DoA) ensures proper screening before approval.
  • Approved entries alone will be considered for Posting Runs.

Solution Architecture:

The proposed solution will work on triggering an approval workflow when the user creates a Recurring Document Entry through FBD1 and when he modifies the rejected Recurring Document Entry through FBD2. Modifications of the Recurring Document Template when it is in Approval is prevented through Custom Control Error Mechanism.

Process Flow:

Development Process:

Step – 1: Create a Recurring Document Entry Template in FBD1 T-Code. The Entry must be blocked, so that only upon complete approval of workflow block shall be removed and would process for posting.

For this purpose, we must enhance the standard procedure through Business Transaction Events. To identify the suitable BTE’s that can be used for our purpose, we can use SE37 transaction with the FM “PC_FUNCTION_FIND”. Keep a Session Breakpoint in the FM and run the process. The Parameter “I_EVENT” will provide the BTE numbers from which we can choose the suitable one for our purpose.

NOTE: For Blocking the Document while creation, through the above-mentioned process we came to the BTE as “SAMPLE_INTERFACE_00001025”.

Step – 2: Take a copy of the FM “SAMPLE_INTERFACE_00001025” in SE37. I have kept the name as “ZFI_BKDF_DOC_BLOCK_FM”.

Copy and paste the below code inside the FM.

DATA:
     lv_memory_bkdf TYPE char30 VALUE '(SAPMF05A)BKDF',
     lv_memory_bkdf1 TYPE char30 VALUE '(SAPMF05L)BKDF',
     wa_bkpf TYPE bkpf.
FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_bkdf> TYPE bkdf.
READ TABLE t_bkpf into wa_bkpf index 1.
    If sy-tcode = 'FBD1' or sy-tcode = 'FBD9'.
      ASSIGN (lv_memory_bkdf) TO <lfs_bkdf>.
    elseif sy-tcode = 'FBD2'.
      ASSIGN (lv_memory_bkdf1) TO <lfs_bkdf>.
    endif.
   IF <lfs_bkdf> IS ASSIGNED AND t_bkpf-bukrs EQ '9000'.
    <lfs_bkdf>-blocked_for_posting = abap_true.
    <lfs_bkdf>-XDELT = abap_true.
  ENDIF.

NOTE:

  • Here “BLOCKED_FOR_POSTING” & “XDELT (Deletion_Indicator) are the fields for which value has been to “X” for blocking the document.
  • The runtime values of these fields are fetched from previous procedures through Memory Assignment.

For FBD1 & FBD2, the standard procedural program varies (See Data Declaration).

Step – 3: Once the Recurring Entry Document is blocked and saved, our custom workflow must get triggered. Before proceeding to Workflow, we must create a custom class where the Event, Methods necessary for the workflow operation would reside.

  1. Go to the T-code SE24 and create a custom class. I had created in the name “ZCL_FI_RECURRING_WF”.  In the Interfaces tab, provide the interfaces as shown in the below image. These interfaces are necessary for Object instantiation & Workflow Operations.
  2. In the Events Tab, I have given the event name as “CREATED” as shown below.
    The event parameter is “ZST_WF_REC_JOUR”. It is a custom table type referring a custom structure "     ZFI_ST_RECJOUR_ENTRY”.

    Structure:
  3. In the Attributes tab, Object Key is specified as “LV_OBJKEY” as shown below.
  4. The standard methods corresponding to the added interfaces, would reflect in the Methods tab as shown below.
  5. Now we can add our custom methods. The following are the methods that I created for my requirement as shown below.
    M_FETCH_APPROVER – Fetching Approvers and Sending Approval Notifications.
    M_UNBLOCK_POSTING – Unblocking the Recurring Entry Document after Complete Approval.
    M_COMPLETE_MAIL – Notification Mail to Initiator after Complete Approval / Rejection in any level.
  6. The individual method procedures and its parameters are below.
    M_FETCH_APPROVER:
    Parameters:

    Here, “ZTT_FI_WF_AGENT” table type is a single field for storing the agents (Image represents structure of the Table Type).
     “ZFI_RECUR_MAT_APP_TT” table type is for storing agent information (Image represents structure of the Table Type).
DATA:
  gc_subject     TYPE so_obj_des, " Email subject
  gc_email_to    TYPE adr6-smtp_addr, " Valid email
  gs_text        TYPE soli, " Text used into the email body
  gc_type_raw    TYPE so_obj_tp, " Email type
  gc_att_type    TYPE soodk-objtp, " Attachment type
  gc_att_subject TYPE sood-objdes. " Attachment title

  DATA:
  gt_text           TYPE soli_tab, " Table which contains email body text
  gt_attachment_hex TYPE solix_tab, " Table which contains the attached file
  gv_sent_to_all    TYPE os_boolean, " Receive the information if email was sent
  gv_error_message  TYPE string, " Used to get the error message
  go_send_request   TYPE REF TO cl_bcs, " Email object
  go_recipient      TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs, " Who will receive the email
  go_sender         TYPE REF TO cl_sapuser_bcs, " Who is sending the email
  go_document       TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs, " Email body
  gx_bcs_exception  TYPE REF TO cx_bcs,
  wa_agents type ZST_FI_APPWF_AGENT.
  wa_rec_wf_sts type ZFI_REC_WF_ST_DT.

    lv_initiator = lv_initiator_us+2(12).
    lv_counter = lv_counter + 1.

     CALL METHOD zcl_amdp_fi_recurring_wf=>get_initial_approvers
      EXPORTING
        p_mandt      = sy-mandt
        p_compcode   = lv_comp_code
        p_init       = lv_initiator
      IMPORTING
        it_approvers = data(it_approvers).

    sort it_approvers by zlevel .
    delete ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM it_approvers comparing zlevel.
    DESCRIBE TABLE it_approvers LINES lv_counter_max.
    read table it_approvers into data(wa_approver) index 1.
    if sy-subrc = 0.
      lv_init_name = wa_approver-name_text1.
      lv_init_mid = wa_approver-smtp_addr1.
    endif.
    read table it_approvers into wa_approver with key zlevel = lv_counter.
    if sy-subrc = 0.
      concatenate 'US' wa_approver-zuser into wa_agents-agent.
      lv_appr_mid = wa_approver-smtp_addr.
      lv_appr_name = wa_approver-name_text.
      append wa_agents to it_agents.
    endif.

   TRY.
    go_send_request = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
    go_sender = cl_sapuser_bcs=>create( 'SAP_WFRT' ).
    go_send_request->set_sender( i_sender = go_sender ).

  DATA(lv_belnr1) = lv_belnr.
  SHIFT lv_belnr1 LEFT DELETING LEADING '0'.

  CONCATENATE 'Approve Jounral Entry' lv_comp_code '-' lv_belnr1 '-' lv_gjahr INTO gc_subject SEPARATED BY space.

  gs_text-line = '<HTML><BODY>'.
  APPEND gs_text to gt_text[].
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  concatenate 'Dear' lv_appr_name into gs_text-line SEPARATED BY SPACE.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR : gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  concatenate 'Recurring Journal Entry' lv_belnr1 '-'  lv_comp_code '-' lv_gjahr 'is waiting for your approval.' into gs_text-line separated by space .
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR : gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = 'Please review the details using the provided link.'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR : gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = 'This is a system generated mail. Please do not reply to this.'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = 'Thanks and Regards,'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = lv_init_name.
  APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  gs_text-line = '</HTML></BODY>'.
  APPEND gs_text to gt_text[].
  CLEAR: gs_text.

  go_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
                    i_type    = 'HTM'
                    i_text    = gt_text
                    i_subject = gc_subject ).

  go_send_request->set_document( go_document ).

*  loop at it_approvers into wa_approver.
    go_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( lv_appr_mid ).
    go_send_request->add_recipient(
          EXPORTING
            i_recipient = go_recipient
            i_express   = abap_true
        ).
*  endloop.

  gv_sent_to_all = go_send_request->send( i_with_error_screen = abap_true ).
  COMMIT WORK.
  CATCH cx_bcs INTO gx_bcs_exception.
    gv_error_message = gx_bcs_exception->get_text( ).
    WRITE gv_error_message.

ENDTRY.

IF LV_COUNTER > LV_COUNTER_MAX.
  LV_EXIT = 'X'.
ELSE.
  LV_EXIT = 'Y'.
ENDIF.

wa_rec_wf_sts-doc_no = lv_belnr1.
wa_rec_wf_sts-comp_code = lv_comp_code.
wa_rec_wf_sts-fis_year = lv_gjahr.
wa_rec_wf_sts-status = 'In-process'.

MODIFY ZFI_REC_WF_ST_DT from wa_rec_wf_sts.
COMMIT WORK.

M_UNBLOCK_POSTING:

Parameters:

    DATA:
      lt_bkdf       TYPE TABLE OF bkdf,
      lt_bkpf       TYPE TABLE OF bkpf,
      lt_dummy_bseg TYPE TABLE OF bseg,
      lt_dummy_bsec TYPE TABLE OF bsec,
      lt_dummy_bsed TYPE TABLE OF bsed,
      lt_dummy_bset TYPE TABLE OF bset.

    SELECT SINGLE *
    FROM bkpf
    INTO (ls_bkpf)
    WHERE bukrs = _bkdf-bukrs
    AND   belnr = _bkdf-belnr
    AND   gjahr = _bkdf-gjahr.

    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      " Read blocked recurring entry data
      SELECT SINGLE *
        FROM bkdf
        INTO (ls_bkdf)
        WHERE bukrs = _bkdf-bukrs
        AND   belnr = _bkdf-belnr
        AND   gjahr = _bkdf-gjahr.

      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        " Remove Block
        ls_bkdf-blocked_for_posting = abap_false.
        ls_bkdf-xdelt = abap_false.
        " Add records
        APPEND ls_bkpf TO lt_bkpf.
        APPEND ls_bkdf TO lt_bkdf.
        " Update document
        CALL FUNCTION 'CHANGE_DOCUMENT'
          TABLES
            t_bkdf = lt_bkdf
            t_bkpf = lt_bkpf
            t_bsec = lt_dummy_bsec
            t_bsed = lt_dummy_bsed
            t_bseg = lt_dummy_bseg
            t_bset = lt_dummy_bset.
        IF sy-subrc = 0.
          COMMIT WORK.
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

M_COMPLETE_MAIL:

Parameters:

        DATA: gc_subject     TYPE so_obj_des, " Email subject
          gc_email_to    TYPE adr6-smtp_addr, " Valid email
          gs_text        TYPE soli, " Text used into the email body
          gc_type_raw    TYPE so_obj_tp, " Email type
          gc_att_type    TYPE soodk-objtp, " Attachment type
          gc_att_subject TYPE sood-objdes. " Attachment title
    DATA:
          gt_text           TYPE soli_tab, " Table which contains email body text
          gt_attachment_hex TYPE solix_tab, " Table which contains the attached file
          gv_sent_to_all    TYPE os_boolean, " Receive the information if email was sent
          gv_error_message  TYPE string, " Used to get the error message
          go_send_request   TYPE REF TO cl_bcs, " Email object
          go_recipient      TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs, " Who will receive the email
          go_sender         TYPE REF TO cl_sapuser_bcs, " Who is sending the email
          go_document       TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs, " Email body
          gx_bcs_exception  TYPE REF TO cx_bcs.

    DATA: e_reason                 TYPE swcont-value,
          reason_txt               TYPE swcont-value,
          l_lines                  TYPE i,
          object_content           TYPE TABLE OF solisti1,
          subcontainer_all_objects TYPE TABLE OF swr_cont,
          lv_wa_reason             LIKE LINE OF subcontainer_all_objects,
          lv_no_att                TYPE  sy-index,
          document_id              TYPE sofolenti1-doc_id,
          return_code              TYPE  sy-subrc,
          ifs_xml_container        TYPE  xstring,
          ifs_xml_container_schema TYPE  xstring,
          simple_container         TYPE TABLE OF swr_cont,
          message_lines            TYPE TABLE OF swr_messag,
          message_struct           TYPE TABLE OF swr_mstruc,
          subcontainer_bor_objects TYPE TABLE OF swr_cont.

    if lv_flag ne 'R'.
       TRY.

          go_send_request = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
          go_sender = cl_sapuser_bcs=>create( 'SAP_WFRT' ).
          go_send_request->set_sender( i_sender = go_sender ).

CONCATENATE 'Recurring Entry Approved:' lv_comp_code lv_belnr lv_gjahr INTO gc_subject SEPARATED BY space.

          gs_text-line = '<HTML><BODY>'.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          CONCATENATE 'Dear' lv_init_name ',' INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          CONCATENATE 'Recurring Journal Entry' lv_belnr lv_comp_code lv_gjahr 'has been completely approved.' INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          CONCATENATE 'This is a system generated mail.' 'Please do not reply to this.' INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          gs_text-line = '</BODY></HTML>'.
          APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
          CLEAR: gs_text.

          go_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
                            i_type    = 'HTM'
                            i_text    = gt_text
                            i_subject = gc_subject ).

          go_send_request->set_document( go_document ).


          go_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( lv_init_mid ).
          "Add recipient to send request
          go_send_request->add_recipient(
            EXPORTING
              i_recipient = go_recipient
              i_express   = abap_true
          ).

          "Send email and get the result
          gv_sent_to_all = go_send_request->send( i_with_error_screen = abap_true ).
          COMMIT WORK.
        CATCH cx_bcs INTO gx_bcs_exception.
          gv_error_message = gx_bcs_exception->get_text( ).
          WRITE gv_error_message.
      ENDTRY.

    else.
      DATA: lv_user TYPE bapibname-bapibname.
      DATA: ls_address TYPE bapiaddr3.
      DATA: lt_return TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.

      lv_user = lv_actual_agent+2(12).

            CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_USER_GET_DETAIL'
              EXPORTING
                username = lv_user
              IMPORTING
                address  = ls_address
              TABLES
                return   = lt_return[].

           CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_WAPI_READ_CONTAINER'
            EXPORTING
              workitem_id              = lv_workitem_id
              language                 = sy-langu
              user                     = sy-uname
            IMPORTING
              return_code              = return_code
              ifs_xml_container        = ifs_xml_container
              ifs_xml_container_schema = ifs_xml_container_schema
            TABLES
              simple_container         = simple_container
              message_lines            = message_lines
              message_struct           = message_struct
              subcontainer_bor_objects = subcontainer_bor_objects
              subcontainer_all_objects = subcontainer_all_objects.
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       Initialize
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
          lv_no_att = 0.
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       Read the _ATTACH_OBJECTS element
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
          LOOP AT subcontainer_all_objects INTO lv_wa_reason
                                           WHERE element = 'DECISION_NOTE'."'_ATTACH_OBJECTS'.
            lv_no_att = lv_no_att + 1.
            document_id = lv_wa_reason-value.
          ENDLOOP.
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       Read the SOFM Document
*      &-----------------------------------------------------------------------------*
          IF document_id IS NOT INITIAL.
            REFRESH : object_content.
            CALL FUNCTION 'SO_DOCUMENT_READ_API1'
              EXPORTING
                document_id                = document_id
*               FILTER                     = 'X '
*       IMPORTING
*               DOCUMENT_DATA              =
              TABLES
*               OBJECT_HEADER              =
                object_content             = object_content
*               OBJECT_PARA                =
*               OBJECT_PARB                =
*               ATTACHMENT_LIST            =
*               RECEIVER_LIST              =
*               CONTENTS_HEX               =
              EXCEPTIONS
                document_id_not_exist      = 1
                operation_no_authorization = 2
                x_error                    = 3
                OTHERS                     = 4.
            IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
              CLEAR : l_lines.
              DESCRIBE TABLE object_content LINES l_lines.
              IF l_lines LT 5.
                READ TABLE object_content INTO reason_txt INDEX 1.
                IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
                  e_reason =  reason_txt.
                ENDIF.
              ENDIF.
            ENDIF.
          ENDIF.

            TRY.
                "Create send request
                go_send_request = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
                "Email FROM...
                go_sender = cl_sapuser_bcs=>create( 'SAP_WFRT' ).
                "Add sender to send request
                go_send_request->set_sender( i_sender = go_sender ).

                CONCATENATE 'Recurring Entry Rejected:' lv_comp_code lv_belnr lv_gjahr INTO gc_subject SEPARATED BY space.

                gs_text-line = '<HTML><BODY>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                CONCATENATE 'Dear' lv_init_name ',' INTO gs_text-ine SEPARATED BY space.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                CONCATENATE 'Journal Entry' lv_belnr lv_comp_code lv_gjahr 'has been rejeted by.' ls_address-fullname INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                CONCATENATE 'Rejection Reason:' e_reason INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                CONCATENATE 'This is a system generated mail.' 'Please do not reply to this.' INTO gs_text-line SEPARATED BY space.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                gs_text-line = '<br></br>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text.
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                gs_text-line = '</BODY></HTML>'.
                APPEND gs_text TO gt_text[].
                CLEAR: gs_text.

                go_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
                                  i_type    = 'HTM'
                                  i_text    = gt_text
*                          i_length  = '12'
                                  i_subject = gc_subject ).

                go_send_request->set_document( go_document ).
                go_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( lv_init_mid ).
                "Add recipient to send request
                go_send_request->add_recipient(
                  EXPORTING
                    i_recipient = go_recipient
                    i_express   = abap_true
                ).
                "Send email and get the result
                gv_sent_to_all = go_send_request->send( i_with_error_screen = abap_true ).
                COMMIT WORK.
              CATCH cx_bcs INTO gx_bcs_exception.
                gv_error_message = gx_bcs_exception->get_text( ).
                WRITE gv_error_message.
            ENDTRY.
    endif.
  endmethod.​

NOTE: Notification sent upon Complete Approval or upon Rejection in any one level is incorporated in the same method.

Thus, the class and methods required for the workflow operation has been completed successfully.

Step – 4: Now we need to trigger the Workflow once the Recurring Document is created in FBD1. For which we need to find the suitable BTE (For finding the BTE, refer the process mentioned in Step-2). The BTE for this requirement is “SAMPLE_INTERFACE_00001030”.
Copy this FM in SE37 to a custom one and write the below code.

IF sy-tcode = 'FBD1' OR sy-tcode = 'FBD9' .                 "Recurring Journal Entry Workflow Event Trigger

DATA: lv_objtype          TYPE sibftypeid,
      lv_event            TYPE sibfevent,
      lv_objkey           TYPE sibfinstid,
      lr_event_parameters TYPE REF TO if_swf_ifs_parameter_container,
      lv_param_name       TYPE swfdname,
      ls_recjour_input    TYPE zfi_st_recjour_entry.

CONSTANTS: lc_objtype    TYPE sibftypeid VALUE 'ZCL_FI_9000_RECURRING_WF',
           lc_event      TYPE sibfevent VALUE 'CREATED',
           lc_param_name TYPE swfdname VALUE 'ZST_WF_REC_JOUR'.

      lv_objtype = lc_objtype.
      lv_event   = lc_event.
      lv_param_name = lc_param_name.

      ls_recjour_input-wa_bkdf = i_bkdf.
      ls_recjour_input-wa_bkpf = t_bkpf.
      ls_recjour_input-wa_bsec = t_bsec.
      ls_recjour_input-wa_bsed = t_bsed.
      ls_recjour_input-wa_bseg = t_bseg.
      ls_recjour_input-wa_bset = t_bset.
      ls_recjour_input-tcode =  sy-tcode .

      concatenate ls_recjour_input-wa_bkpf-belnr ls_recjour_input-wa_bkpf-bukrs ls_recjour_input-wa_bkpf-gjahr into lv_objkey.

      CALL METHOD cl_swf_evt_event=>get_event_container
        EXPORTING
          im_objcateg  = cl_swf_evt_event=>mc_objcateg_cl
          im_objtype   = lv_objtype
          im_event     = lv_event
        RECEIVING
          re_reference = lr_event_parameters.

      TRY.
          CALL METHOD lr_event_parameters->set
            EXPORTING
              name  = lv_param_name
              value = ls_recjour_input.

        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_cont_access_denied .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_elem_access_denied .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_elem_not_found .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_elem_type_conflict .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_unit_type_conflict .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_elem_def_invalid .
        CATCH cx_swf_cnt_container .
     ENDTRY.

       TRY.
          CALL METHOD cl_swf_evt_event=>raise
            EXPORTING
              im_objcateg        = cl_swf_evt_event=>mc_objcateg_cl
              im_objtype         = lv_objtype
              im_event           = lv_event
              im_objkey          = lv_objkey
              im_event_container = lr_event_parameters.
          COMMIT WORK.
        CATCH cx_swf_evt_invalid_objtype .
        CATCH cx_swf_evt_invalid_event .
      ENDTRY.

      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
*        WRITE: 'Error occurred while triggering workflow event.' ##NO_TEXT.
      ENDIF.
   ENDIF.
   ENDIF.

STEP – 5: Now we need to create tasks in PFTC, for the methods that were created in STEP-3. This is a very normal procedure of creating the tasks that are to be consumed by the activity in the Workflow Template.

NOTE: For the User Decision Step, take a copy of the standard decision step (TS00008267) and do the necessary modifications and perform agent assignment. Once done, capture the Agent Assignment in a Customizing TR by running the report RHMOVE30 in SE38.

 STEP – 6: I had created Flexible Workflow for this requirement. Open the transaction “SWDD_SCENARIO”. Create the Workflow containers with reference to all the event and the method parameters in the Custom Class created in STEP-3. 

  1. In the Process Data Tab, provide the class name and the associated event. Do the necessary event binding and activate the event as shown below.

    NOTE: Caputre the Event Activation in the same customizing TR as mentioned in STEP - 5.
  2. In the Activities Tab, create the activities for the tasks that were created in STEP-5. Include the User Decision Activity, in the list of activities.

    NOTE: “DISPLAY_TCODE” is an activity which we have created for accessing the T-Code “FBD1”.
  3. In the Conditions Tab, we have created one condition. This condition is for stopping the Workflow upon completion of all the agents.
  4. When we create Flexible Workflow, we must create a customized Runtime Class. This Runtime Class helps us to utilize “Instance Methods” upon available events. We need to create a Subclass of the standard Runtime Class “CL_SWF_FLEX_IFS_RUN_APPL_BASE”. Then we can redefine the methods necessary for our purpose. There would be several standard methods which would be useful for several scenarios.
    NOTE: For our process, we can redefine below methods, as shown below.

    NOTE: First method would get triggered just before the Workflow is about to complete. Second method would trigger upon completion of each activity. Use TaskId as filter, such that procedure only for suitable tasks would run.

    Inside the Result Callback method, utilize the mail notification methods created in STEP-3.

  5. Activate the Workflow. Since we are implementing Flexible Workflow, we need to activate the scenario. Follow the path mentioned below.
    SPRO -> ABAP Platform -> Application Server -> Business Management -> SAP Business Workflow -> Flexible Workflow -> Scenario Activation. 

    NOTE: Provide the Custom Workflow Number and activate the scenario. You can capture this in TR & transport to further landscape.

  6. Now we need to Login to the FIORI launchpad. As Pre-requisites implement “Manage Workflows” & “Manage Workflow Scenarios” applications.
    NOTE: In the “Manage Workflows” application create the Workflow, along with the sequence of the steps in which the workflow must be executed.
    Manage Workflow Header:

    Execution Steps:


    Here the maximum level is 4. However, if we keep the maximum approver level less than 4 also, the Workflow will stop at the designated maximum level. This is based the Step-Condition indicated as Final Level in the above screenshot.

    Once created, open the “Manage Workflow Scenarios” application and export the scenario. This will get downloaded as a XML file in the system.

  7. Move the Customizing TR and import the downloaded XML file into the testing landscape and to further landscapes.
  8. Upon Complete approval of the workflow, the “BLOCKED_FOR_POSTING” & “XDELT (Deletion_Indicator) fields would be reset. This makes the document eligible for further posting through Perioidic Job Scheduling in F.14 Transaction.
  9. Incase if the approver rejects the document, he can provide the Reason for Rejection. This will be sent along with the Rejection Notification to the Initiator of the Workflow (i.e Recurring Entry Document Creator). 
  10. The Document Creator can modify according to the Rejection Comments and save the document. The Workflow would get triggered again for another approval cycle.
    NOTE: I had designed the solution such that the document cannot be edited, unless it is fully approved / rejected. This is an optional solution to be incorporated. 

 Conclusion:
Recurring entries simplify financial operations, but without governance, they can introduce compliance risks. By integrating FBD1 with an approval workflow, organizations can achieve the right balance between automation and financial control, ensuring accuracy, transparency, and audit readiness.

entry.title: SAP CI to ABAP Integration: Seamless XML to Internal Table Mapping
entry.content:
In many SAP CI integrations, XML responses are received from external systems and passed to the ABAP layer. A common requirement is to convert this XML data into an internal table in ABAP.

There are two common approaches:

  1. Use XSLT mapping in SAP Backend (SAP ECC/S4HANA) to transform the XML into the required structure. ( You can follow blog https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/xslt-transformations-xml-2-abap-a-working-example-with-deep-structures/ba-p/13370654 )
  2. Prepare the XML in SAP CI in ABAP XML format so that it can be directly converted into an internal table using ABAP transformation.

This blog explains the second and simpler approach.

Why Use ABAP XML Format?

If the XML follows the ABAP XML structure with the required tags:

  • <abap version="1.0">
  • <values>

Then ABAP can directly deserialize the XML into an internal table using:

CALL TRANSFORMATION id

This avoids complex XSLT mappings and simplifies the integration.

Sample XML That Can Be Directly Converted

Below is a sample XML payload prepared in SAP CI that can be directly converted into an internal table in ABAP:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<abap version="1.0">
    <values>
        <OUTPUT>
            <ZCON_PAC_LOCATION>
                <LOCATIONCODE>US</LOCATIONCODE>
                <PXCREATEDATETIME>2010-01-29 15:44:01</PXCREATEDATETIME>
                <PXCREATEOPNAME>Fname Lname</PXCREATEOPNAME>
                <PXCREATEOPERATOR>test@mail.com</PXCREATEOPERATOR>
            </ZCON_PAC_LOCATION>
        </OUTPUT>
    </values>
</abap>

Important: The tags <abap version="1.0"> and <values> are mandatory for ABAP deserialization using the ID transformation.

Generating the XSD

The simplest way to generate the XSD is:

  1. Create an RFC-enabled function module with the required ABAP structure.
  2. Access the WSDL using: http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/soap/wsdl11?services=<RFC_Name>&sap-client=<client>
  3. Copy the relevant schema portion from the WSDL.

Sample XSD

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
           xmlns:asx="http://www.sap.com/abapxml"
           xmlns:sap="http://www.sap.com/sapxsl"
           elementFormDefault="qualified"
           attributeFormDefault="unqualified">

    <xs:element name="abap" type="AbapType"/>

    <xs:complexType name="AbapType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="values" type="ValuesType"/>
        </xs:sequence>
        <xs:attribute name="version" type="xs:string" use="required"/>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="ValuesType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="OUTPUT" type="OutputType"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="OutputType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="ZCN_PAC_LOCATION"
                        type="PacLocation"
                        minOccurs="0"
                        maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="PacLocation">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="LOCATIONCODE" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEDATETIME" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEOPNAME" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEOPERATOR" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>
</xs:schema>

SAP CI Mapping:


In SAP CI, map the response so that:

  • The root node is abap with attribute version="1.0".
  • Under it, include values.
  • Under values, include your wrapper node such as OUTPUT.
  • Inside the wrapper, repeat the structure node for each record.

Once structured correctly, no additional transformation is required in ABAP.

ABAP code :

"Data Declarations
DATA: lo_http_client TYPE REF TO if_http_client,
      lv_request     TYPE xstring,
      lv_response    TYPE xstring.
"Convert Internal Table to XML (Request to CPI)
CALL TRANSFORMATION id
  SOURCE pac_project_department_request = lt_request[]
  RESULT XML lv_request.

"Create HTTP Client
cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING
        destination              = 'DestinationName'
    IMPORTING
        client                   = lo_http_client
    EXCEPTIONS
        argument_not_found       = 1
        destination_not_found    = 2
        destination_no_authority = 3
        plugin_not_active        = 4
        internal_error           = 5
        OTHERS                   = 6 ).

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
"Suitable error handling
ENDIF.
"Disable logon popup:
lo_http_client->propertytype_logon_popup = lo_http_client->co_disabled.
"Send HTTP Request
lo_http_client->request->set_method( 'POST' ).
"Set xml request body
lo_http_client->request->set_data( data = lv_request ).
lo_http_client->send(
    EXCEPTIONS
        http_communication_failure = 1
        http_invalid_state         = 2
        http_processing_failed     = 3
        http_invalid_timeout       = 4
        OTHERS                     = 5 ).

IF sy-subrc = 0.
    lo_http_client->receive(
        EXCEPTIONS
            http_communication_failure = 1
            http_invalid_state         = 2
            http_processing_failed     = 3
            OTHERS                     = 5 ).
ENDIF.
lo_http_client->close( ).
lv_response = lo_http_client->response->get_data( ).
"Convert XML Response to Internal Table
CALL TRANSFORMATION id
  SOURCE XML lv_response
  RESULT output = et_pac_departments.

ABAP Dictionary Objects

  • Internal table: ET_PAC_DEPARTMENTS
  • Table type: ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY
  • Line type: ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT

The internal table ET_PAC_DEPARTMENTS is defined with table type ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY.

The table type ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY refers to the line structure ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT.

The structure ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT contains the following fields:

  • LOCATIONCODE (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEDATETIME (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEOPNAME (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEOPERATOR (TYPE string)

Since the XML structure matches the ABAP structure exactly, the CALL TRANSFORMATION id statement automatically deserializes the XML and populates the internal table.

 

Conclusion

By preparing the XML payload in SAP CI using ABAP XML format with the correct structure and mandatory tags, we can directly convert the XML into an internal table in ABAP using CALL TRANSFORMATION id.

This approach simplifies SAP CI integration scenarios and reduces transformation complexity significantly.

entry.title: Why SELECT returns different rows when querying a CDS View on MSEG versus MSEG directly
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • Why the Row Count Differs
  • Key Takeaways

 

Introduction

If you’ve recently worked with S/4HANA and created a custom CDS view on a standard table such as MSEG (*), you may have noticed something surprising:

Even if the CDS view is a simple SELECT FROM MSEG without any WHERE clause and there's no CDS Access Control implied, the number of rows returned by ABAP SELECT * FROM ZCDS_E_VIEW may not match the results of ABAP SELECT * FROM MSEG.

(*) You may find other tables like MSEG in the note 2206980 - Material Inventory Managment: change of data model in S/4HANA - SAP for Me.

 

Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_V_MSEG'
define view ZCDS_E_MSEG as select from MSEG
{
  *
}

NB: a DDIC-based CDS view is used here to make the example simple, but as it's obsolete since ABAP 7.57 CDS view entities should be used instead.

 

ABAP SELECT on MSEG

SELECT * FROM MSEG
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_mseg).
This example assumes there are some lines in MSEG for MBLNR = '5000000000'.
This ABAP SELECT may return more rows than the below ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG.
 

ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG

SELECT * FROM zcds_e_mseg
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_cds_mseg).
Even though this query selects all rows from the CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG (which is based on the physical MSEG table without any WHERE), the number of rows may be fewer than the ABAP SELECT on MSEG.
 

Why the Row Count Differs

  1. The table MSEG exists physically in the database but is no longer maintained

    • In S/4HANA, the real material document data lives in MATDOC.

    • The legacy table MSEG still exists in the database with the original ECC data, but is no more updated by S/4HANA transactions.

  2. ABAP SELECT on MSEG is redirected to the replacement object

    • When ABAP code does SELECT * FROM MSEG, SAP automatically redirects the query to the CDS replacement view NSDM_E_MSEG, which reads MATDOC.



      NB: NSDM_E_MSEG filters MATDOC using WHERE record_type = 'MDOC' but it's not implied in the problem discussed here.
      You can see in the SQL Trace (ST05) how ABAP SELECT ... FROM MSEG ... is automatically using the replacement object:

    • This redirection ensures that old programs continue to work correctly and return all material document lines, i.e. those lines migrated and updated in S/4HANA live.

  3. Custom CDS views on MSEG read the physical table directly

    • Any CDS view defined on MSEG does not use the replacement object, so it reads the actual content of the MSEG table, which are the data migrated from ECC, but are no more updated in S/4HANA.

    • If the table MSEG is empty in your system, it's because the Obsolete Data Handling tool has run (since S/4HANA 2023, it's the transaction code SODH, which has superseded ODH_DATA_PROCESSING). Reference: SAP S/4HANA Upgrade Guide, section Cleaning Up Obsolete Data After the Upgrade.

 

✅Key Takeaways

  • Do not rely on MSEG for new development; always use MATDOC or NSDM replacement views such as NSDM_E_MSEG.

  • NEVER use the old tables in your custom CDS views because they contain obsolete data.

  • After you have successfully tested and validated the upgrade results, you can clean up the obsolete data with the Obsolete Data Handling tool, to free space and to avoid the confusion and panic among developers.

entry.title: Future SAP apps will be transient - prove me wrong!
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • What is a transient app
  • Prompting
  • Benefit to user
  • Objections and Concerns
  • Cost of code
  • Training
  • Consistency across users and time
  • Scenario
  • Traditional apps (today)
  • Transient apps (future)
  • Summary

Introduction

Predicting the future is a fool’s errand! Things change at a fast pace, so we generally do not try and predict the winner of a sporting tournament 10 years in advance – unless we’re trying to stoke discussion. That’s my goal with this post!

 

What is a transient app

A transient app is an app that is written on-the-fly and lasts only during the session in which it is used. It is then removed from all memory as if it never existed. The concept of a transient app arose in the AI community around 2024/25 and is well described by a comparison to traditional apps as follows:

 

If a traditional app is like a restaurant you must travel to, a transient app is like a personal chef who appears in your kitchen, cooks exactly what you want, and cleans up so perfectly you wouldn't know they were there.

 Important:  Transient apps exist in a new world where software creation approximates being both free and instant!

In the general world of AI, a transient app can pull data from various sources and so is not restricted to a single system like SAP.  Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP) is currently the best example of how this is done. We will, however, assume an entirely SAP ecosphere. Hence, we are considering what could be called SAP transient apps – Lets call them STAs for short.

Prompting

Prompting is an important concept in the world of AI including our future STA scenario. There must always be an interface with this SAP AI we’re imagining where a user can give “prompts”. This may be text-based (probably how you currently use your favourite LLM) or a verbal prompt or something else ( Neuralink?  😯 ) .

 

Benefit to user

For now, let’s ignore all objections to whether an STA is possible and look only at why a user would even want such a thing. Why not just have a standard interface that everyone uses i.e. what we have today?

Let’s look at some reasons using the simple example of a timesheet STA:

  1. The AI will learn to know what a user’s taste is in terms of UI. This is tweaked as user’s respond with prompts informing how a presented UI can be improved . One user may prefer a table like interface . Another might prefer tabs for each day with various colours, public holidays flashing, birthday reminders etc. Yet another might prefer a more wizard-like approach. Remember this is a simple example. A more complicated app will have more possible UI variations.

      2.  A UI may not be needed at all!  A user may be happy to give the prompt “log that I worked on all workdays this week”.  A UI-less STA can be generated that does this task. I'd use this option!

 

Objections and Concerns

This is the big one! Why STAs might seem like a pipedream – and why I think they are not.

 

Cost of code

The most fundamental hurdle may be our understanding of the cost of code. In our current world code requires human expertise and therefore has dollar costs and time costs. It is too valuable to throw out once its created.

 

Think of the darning kits that some of us can remember being around many years ago. They contained what was needed to repair a hole in a sock. Once socks became cheaper it became easier to “just buy a new pair” than sit down and spend time repairing. Just as socks have lost their value ( but not importance ) to us,  AI will make code less valuable ( but still important ) because it’s both quick and cheap to create.

 

This concept of code being created instantly is reminiscent of SAP’s move to HANA that imagined a world where there were no time costs to access data. Turns out that we still need to care about optimisation in DB selects because the time costs are not absolutely zero. However, the speed is sufficiently fast that our S4 ecosphere works well with the reduced attention on data select loads. No doubt there will be such nuances ahead where we find code to be not entirely “free and instant” but close enough to make a difference.

 

Training

How do you train people to use something when you don’t know exactly how the UI they will use looks? I’ll make the answer to this one short. The user can be walked through any given UI presented to them by the AI itself (like a chatbot). On the first few uses of a UI to do a particular task they will probably request changes to the UI to suite their preferences. This will be remembered by the AI so that the user is not always seeing a different UI for a task that is done repeatedly. Even a brand-new task can result in a UI based on preferences requested by the user on previous tasks e.g. a user might prefer that wizard style flow in the UI. The AI gets to know you!

 

Consistency across users and time        

This is the question of whether one transient app is doing exactly the same thing as another transient app. It is a question more likely to be asked by an SAP expert than a user.

How do we know:

  1. That any 2 users are subject to the exact same business rules if they are using different STAs?
  2. Why certain data was changed in a certain way at a certain point in time? How  can we reproduce that to either audit or debug an issue if the STA that created the data is gone?

These concerns arise because we think in terms of traditional apps (whether Fiori apps, Web Dynpro apps or WEBGUI transactions).

Our traditional apps have business rules embedded in them! Even though we claim to abstract them away using BRF, public methods or something else the reality is that they are embedded in our code throughout. Let’s look at a simple example to contrast the current traditional world with the transient world of the future.

 

Scenario

Materials of type “Dangerous Goods” will be added to the system. Only users with the role DGA (Dangerous Goods Approved) will be able to work with these materials. Anywhere, such a material is added to a document (like when creating a Purchase Order) the user must for legal reasons check a checkbox saying they have considered storage implications.

 

Traditional apps (today)

The Functional consultant will put the scenario above in a spec. They will then track down all UIs that allow a user  to create a document that uses materials. They may find (for our example) 3 such apps (the PO create Fiori app, a PO create transaction and a Stock Transfer Order app to move materials between warehouses). They will then instruct security to create the role and assign to a list of people. They will ask the technical team to restrict materials available in the 3 apps to non-dangerous goods for unauthorised users – this can be done on the backend for all 3 apps (exits in DPC classes, BADIs, implicit enhancements etc).

On the frontend, developers will be asked to add the checkbox whenever a dangerous good is selected by authorised users and to confirm it has been checked before allowing save/submit of the document.

Note that this new requirement is embedded into both backend and frontend code! If a new custom app is created that works with materials, the functional consultant will need to remember this requirement to add it in. If it is not the same functional consultant then hopefully they’ve read a document somewhere that told them of this requirement (or it was in a knowledge transfer they were given 😆😆).

Transient apps (future)

A functional consultant will declare the new requirement via an AI prompt. The prompt will basically be the scenario paragraph above. When the AI then asks exactly which users should have the new authorisation for dangerous goods the response could be something like  “everyone in the current role ABC plus those in role DEF but don’t make a composite of these roles as this might change. Just create a new DGA role containing the current union of ABC and DEF”.

The rules will be extracted completely into a rules engine. This rules engine does not need to be human readable (like a BRF). That said, any authorised person can query this rules engine, and the results will be presented in a transient SAP app which will not only be human readable but optimised for that particular user's taste.

Any user creating a PO, say, will be subject to the rules and they will be enforced by the transient PO Creation app they use in their session.

We see a secret to the transient app – the business rules are completely abstracted. We are left with only 3 persisting objects:

  1. Data (transaction, master & config)
  2. Business engine (containing business rules)
  3. AI engine

The apps themselves don’t “live” anywhere. Some data and all business rules are timestamped. That way we could reproduce a transient app the way it would have run 2 years ago. Sure, the colours and other preferences may look different to what it did to the user that did something 2 years ago but the resulting behaviour is identical. 

Summary

Future SAP users will not be content with a prescribed app layout combined with a handful of themes like Quartz Light or Horizon Dark. They will insist on transient apps creating UIs that precisely match their personal preferences!

 

OK let's see how that ages...

entry.title: HOW TO FETCH DATA FOR THE LATEST RECORD FROM SAP TABLE USING CDS VIEW
entry.content:
Introduction: Fetching latest record from sap table could be tricky and might end up with the incorrect record and you will see why it is so and how to solve it in this blog. Below is a way to fetch data for the latest record from table EKES by looking up the created-on-date (ERDAT) and time (EZEIT) field of the table for a set of key fields and join this to primary tables like EKKO and EKPO.

  • I have the table EKES in sap and I need to fetch the fields EINDT and MENGE for the latest record for every line of purchasing document.
  • Create a CDS view and define it to query the table EKES.
  • Select ebeln and ebelp as the key fields.
  • Concatenate erdat and ezeit and get the max value of this string grouped by ebeln and ebelp.
define view entity ZCDS_TEST as select from EKES 
{ 
key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
max(concat(coalesce(erdat,'00000000'), coalesce(ezeit,'000000'))) as max_date } 
group by ebeln, ebelp
  • This will get the latest record.

  • Coalesce function should be used here to make sure that if any field is empty then the length is maintained to 8+6 = 14 digits, so that we do not get the wrong result.
  • Now we want to fetch the EINDT and MENGE value for this record.
  • If we directly fetch these fields in the view, then it will not return correct data as we will have to add them in the group by clause.

  • Below will be the result if we group by ebeln, ebelp, eindt and menge.

  • Instead, we can concatenate these required fields in the max_date field of the view to avoid the fields in group by clause
define view entity ZCDS_TEST as select from EKES 
{ key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
max(concat(coalesce(erdat,'00000000'), 
concat(coalesce(ezeit,'000000'), 
concat(coalesce(eindt, '00000000'), 
coalesce(menge, 0.000) as abap.char(16))))) as max_date 
} group by ebeln, ebelp
  • The marking in green is the EINDT value and the one in yellow is for MENGE.

  • In the main cds view, join this view with EKKO and EKPO. Get the substring of max_date from position 15 till the next 8 digits. This will be the EINDT value. For MENGE value, get the substring of max_date from the position 23 till the next 16 characters, as menge is 16 digits.
define view entity ZCDS_TEST_JOIN as select from EKKO as a 
left outer join ekpo as b on a.ebeln = b.ebeln 
inner join zcds_test as c on b.ebeln = c.ebeln 
and b.ebelp = c.ebelp 
{ 
key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
a.ernam, 
b.aedat, 
case when coalesce(c.max_date, '') = '' or substring(c.max_date, 15, 8) like '%00000000' then '00000000' else cast(substring(c.max_date, 15, 8)
 as abap.dats) end as eindt, 
case when coalesce(c.max_date, '') = '' or substring(c.max_date, 15, 8) like '%00000000' 
then '0.000' 
else cast(substring(c.max_date, 23, 16) as abap.dats) 
end as menge 
}
  • You can see the eindt and Menge in the below for key combination of ebeln and ebelp.

Conclusion:
